statement.markdown:
Given a string containing uppercase and lowercase letters and spaces, remove consecutive characters that are the same letter, but keep spaces intact.

Example: Input string: *abba ccdd eeee*  should return string: *abc cc dd e*

The method should return a string containing the result. 

The input string will contain at most 10000 characters.  It will contain at least 2 characters.  There will be at least one space in the string.

Hint: You might need to use a pointer and/or use string concatenation.
statement.inputFormat: A string containing uppercase and lowercase letters and spaces.
statement.outputFormat: The string with consecutive characters of the same letter removed.
statement.notes: You can use the built-in str.split() method to process the string, if you prefer.

examples.explanation: Remove consecutive characters that are the same letter, but keep spaces intact.

hints.text: Use a pointer to track the position of the next valid character.

editorial.title: Solution Explanation
editorial.intuition: Use two pointers to track the position of the next valid character and the current character.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute Force approach: iterate through all characters in the string, and check if the current character is the same as the previous character. If they are the same, remove the current character.
editorial.betterSolution: Optimal Solution: Use two pointers to track the position of the next valid character and the current character.
editorial.optimalSolution: Optimal Solution: Use two pointers to track the position of the next valid character and the current character. If the next valid character is not the same as the current character, move the previous character to the position of the next valid character.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Proof of correctness: The optimal solution has a time complexity of O(n), where n is the size of the string.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run: Input string: *abba ccdd eeee*. 1. Initialize the first pointer at position 0, and the second pointer at position 0. 2. Get the first character at position 0 ('a'). 3. Get the next character at position 1 ('b'). 4. Since 'a' and 'b' are not the same, move the previous character 'a' to the position of the next valid character. 5. Repeat the process until the end of the string is reached.
commonMistakes: Not using two pointers to track the position of the next valid character and the current character.
